skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate, edit, upscale, and combine AI images through the inference.sh CLI using hosted models such as FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, ImagineArt, and Topaz.
skill.summary: Create AI images and image edits with hosted inference.sh models from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified a high-risk remote install pattern: the skill tells users to pipe a downloaded script directly into sh. Most Ruby backtick and weak-crypto alerts are false positives from Markdown formatting or unsupported line context, but the skill still requires external CLI execution and network calls to hosted image services.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to download an install script and execute it directly with sh. This bypasses repository review and could run arbitrary remote code if the endpoint or transport is compromised.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present on line 17. This is a known high-risk installation pattern even though the surrounding text claims checksum verification.

medium_findings.title: Networked External Model Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill runs hosted image models through the infsh CLI. Prompts and image URLs can be sent to inference.sh or downstream model providers, which creates privacy, billing, and provider-policy risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples invoke infsh app run against hosted model IDs and include prompt or image URL inputs. This appears intentional for the skill, but it requires explicit user awareness.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Access Granted
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access for infsh commands and provides many command examples. The scope is limited to the infsh CLI, but marketplace users should treat it as external command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples are direct evidence. The risk is moderated because the declared command scope is specific to infsh rather than arbitrary shell use.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Vendor URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, images, installer, and checksum resources. No evidence found that these URLs exfiltrate local secrets, but they create normal external dependency risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and vendor-related. Their intent appears documentary or operational, not malicious, based on the surrounding skill content.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Markdown Backtick Alerts
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick detections are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline code formatting. No Ruby code execution syntax is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced locations are Markdown examples or table cells, not executable Ruby source. The skill contains only SKILL.md, so there is no Ruby runtime context.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Alert
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm at line 3 is not supported by the file content. The only explicit checksum reference found is SHA-256 on line 23, which is not a weak hash for integrity checking.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is metadata text and line 23 references SHA-256 verification. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA-1, or another weak algorithm in the reviewed content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote installer execution with curl piped to sh can execute unaudited code outside the marketplace package.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is present exactly at the cited location and directly invokes sh on downloaded content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hosted Service Command Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The infsh examples submit prompts and image URLs to external hosted apps. This can expose user-provided creative briefs or source images to third parties.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples show prompt and image URL fields being passed to infsh app run. The external service behavior is inherent to the documented workflow.

content.user_title: Create AI Images with inference.sh Models
content.value_statement: Teams need quick access to many image models without learning each provider interface. This skill provides ready CLI patterns for generation, editing, upscaling, and image composition through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Runs text-to-image prompts through inference.sh model app IDs.
Lists image generation models such as FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, ImagineArt, and Topaz.
Shows commands for browsing available image apps from the CLI.
Provides examples for image upscaling with a source image URL.
Provides examples for stitching multiple image URLs horizontally or vertically.
Links to related inference.sh skills and documentation pages.
limitations: Requires the infsh CLI, an inference.sh account, and network access.
Sends prompts, parameters, and image URLs to hosted services.
The documented installer uses a remote shell script and should be reviewed before use.
Does not run image models locally or guarantee provider availability.

use_cases.title: Create Campaign Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate product shots, social graphics, and concept variations from short creative prompts.

use_cases.title: Prototype Game and Product Art
use_cases.description: Explore visual directions with several hosted models before commissioning final assets.

use_cases.title: Improve Existing Image Assets
use_cases.description: Upscale source images or combine multiple images for publishing workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Simple Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a clear product-style image of [subject]. Use a [style] look, [lighting], and [aspect ratio].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Model Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend the best inference.sh image model for [goal]. Explain the tradeoffs for speed, quality, text rendering, and aspect ratio support.

prompt_templates.title: Upscale an Existing Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the upscaling workflow for this image URL: [image URL]. Preserve the original composition and prepare it for [publishing format].

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Visual Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an inference.sh workflow for [campaign or product]. Include model selection, generation prompts, revision steps, upscaling, and final asset checks.

output_examples.input: Create a studio product photo of a ceramic mug for an online store.
output_examples.output: A concise model recommendation, a polished image prompt, suggested aspect ratio, and the infsh workflow to generate the asset.

output_examples.input: Upscale a draft poster image and keep the text readable.
output_examples.output: An upscaling workflow with cautions about source image quality, text preservation, and reviewing the final image before publication.

output_examples.input: Make a horizontal banner from two existing image URLs.
output_examples.output: A stitching workflow that combines the images in the requested direction and notes the expected network handling of image URLs.

best_practices: Review any remote installer before running it, and prefer verified manual installation when available.
Avoid sending confidential prompts, unpublished images, or customer data to hosted models.
Record model IDs, prompt text, and important parameters for repeatable image revisions.
anti_patterns: Do not run the pipe-to-shell installer in sensitive environments without independent review.
Do not assume generated text inside images is accurate without manual inspection.
Do not use third-party image URLs unless you have rights to process and publish them.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI to call hosted image models over the network.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated high?
faq.answer: The quick start includes a remote installer piped directly to sh, which can execute unaudited code.

faq.question: Can it upscale existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes an example using the Topaz image upscaler with an image URL.

faq.question: Can it combine multiple images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a stitch-images example for combining image URLs in one direction.

faq.question: What data should users avoid sending?
faq.answer: Users should avoid confidential prompts, private source images, customer data, and unlicensed third-party content.
